what: Step 1: Defining file path and processing information
title: Reading & processing flight data
text: First thing define location .TIF file want process. kind files usually pretty big exceed GitHub’s maximum file size limit keep mind managing data. case, stored flight information separate folder locally. file path: continuing, also define 3 inputs read_n_process_flight_data function. First Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM) zone drone flight took place. UTM coordinate system divides world 60 north-south zones, 6 degrees longitude wide. calculate UTM zone flight took place, can use rough longitude flight took place apply formula: \[ UTM_{zone} = \lfloor \frac{Longitude + 180}{6} \rfloor + 1 \] case, average longitude flight used example -119.45 UTM zone : throne package also includes get_utm_zone function perform calculation . use , can input rough longitude flight took place aware value needs -180 180 , unfortunately, Earth flat. Next need define hemisphere flight took place; either north south. case, example flight took place northern hemisphere: Lastly, need define number decimal digits coordinate. Using decimal coordinate system (.e., -119.45 instead coordinate systems like DMS 119° 27' 0" W highly discourage) decimal degree convey certain distance. distances differ longitude latitude degrees become smaller higher latitudes. part documentation package included data set (spatial_resolution_lat_long.csv) visualize pattern. plot generated data express area rectangle sides (representing longitude latitude) specific number decimal digits (.e., 1, 0.1, 0.001 etc.) different latitudes.  recommend spatial resolution 5 decimal places since, latitudes, convey area \(< 1 m^2\) relevant scale vast majority terrestrial animals.
code: filepath <- "C:/Users/ggarc/OneDrive/projects/throne_data/test_flight.tif" utm_zone = floor((-119.45 + 180) / 6) + 1 utm_zone ## [1] 11 get_utm_zone(15.4) ## [1] 33 get_utm_zone(195) ## [1] "Error: Longitude values need to be between -180 & 180" hemisphere <- "north" ## Warning: Using `size` aesthetic for lines was deprecated in ggplot2 3.4.0. ## ℹ Please use `linewidth` instead. decimal_places <- 5

what: Step 2: Reading the flight’s file (.TIF) and transform into a tibble object
title: Reading & processing flight data
text: Using file path defined earlier, can use raster function package name order read file R environment: file load Formal class RasterLayer can actually plotted using base R:  , can use rasterToPoints function raster package transform raster object data.frame: resulting data.frame 3 columns. First x y, equivalent longitude latitude (although different units, Step 3) column called layer equivalent temperature drone captured degrees Celsius. number rows data frame heavily dependent resolution drone’s camera. example flight obtained using DJI Matrice 200 V2 FLIR Zenmuse XT2 thermal imaging camera bit overkill. 10th observations worked just fine. important note majority observations layer column 0. temperature recorded drone exactly 0 drone record data. 0 observations removed reduces size final data.frame (presented tibble ) substantially:

what: Step 3: Transforming coordinates and generating final output
title: Reading & processing flight data
text: pointed earlier, coordinate columns final tibble obtained previous step weird format. Currently, x y defined distance meters western northern edges UTM zone flight took place. correlate information data sources, need transform data decimal coordinate system. , first need define projection string, provide information function project proj4 package perform transformation. read_n_process_flight_data function , happening behind scenes: can project data decimal coordinate units. computationally longest step entire process. Last least need define spatial resolution conducting measurements dependent amount decimal_places defined earlier. read_n_process_flight_data function , happening behind scenes: last summarizing step reduces size data frame point data can already plotted using ggplot tools part posterior analysis. instance, can visualize map using geom_tile geom_raster: Note image plotted exactly one showed earlier plotting raster altering color scheme. Additionally, can already get descriptive metrics thermal landscape can visualize distribution:
code: # generate the string including utm_zone and hemisphere string <-  paste("+proj=utm +zone=", utm_zone," +", hemisphere," +ellps=WGS84 +datum=WGS84 +units=m +no_defs", sep = "") string ## [1] "+proj=utm +zone=11 +north +ellps=WGS84 +datum=WGS84 +units=m +no_defs" # project to decimal coordinates coords <- as.data.frame(project(data.frame(x = df$x, y = df$y), string, inverse = TRUE)) head(coords) ##           x        y ## 1 -119.4595 39.75115 ## 2 -119.4595 39.75115 ## 3 -119.4595 39.75115 ## 4 -119.4595 39.75115 ## 5 -119.4595 39.75115 ## 6 -119.4595 39.75115 # add temperature column to coordinates  coords$temp <- df$layer  # rename x & y columns  data <- coords %>% rename(long = x, lat = y)  # define decimal degrees on latitude and longitude measurements data$long <- as.numeric(format(round(data$long, decimal_places), nsmall = decimal_places)) data$lat <- as.numeric(format(round(data$lat, decimal_places), nsmall = decimal_places))  # get mean temperature for same coordinates  data <- data %>% group_by(lat, long) %>% summarise(temp = mean(temp, na.rm = T)) %>% ungroup() ## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'lat'. You can override using the `.groups` ## argument. data ## # A tibble: 105,045 × 3 ##      lat  long  temp ##    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> ##  1  39.7 -119.  33.9 ##  2  39.7 -119.  31.9 ##  3  39.7 -119.  30.5 ##  4  39.7 -119.  33.6 ##  5  39.7 -119.  35.0 ##  6  39.7 -119.  33.7 ##  7  39.7 -119.  33.7 ##  8  39.7 -119.  32.7 ##  9  39.7 -119.  33.5 ## 10  39.7 -119.  28.1 ## # … with 105,035 more rows data %>%    ggplot(aes(x = long, y = lat, fill = temp)) +   geom_raster() +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "lightgoldenrod1", high = "red4") +   xlab("Longitude") + ylab("Latitude") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = c(0.95, 0.85)) data %>%    ungroup() %>%   ggplot(aes(x = temp)) +   geom_density(fill = "lightgoldenrod", linewidth = 1.25) +   geom_vline(aes(xintercept = mean(temp, na.rm = T)), linewidth = 1.25) +   xlab("Temperature (as captured by drone,°C)") + ylab("Density") +   theme_minimal()

title: Reading & processing .TIF drone files
text: First thing define location .TIF file want process. kind files usually pretty big exceed Git Hub’s maximum file size limit recommend keeping separate data folder. case, file path: addition, also define 3 inputs function. First Universal Transverse Mercator (UTM) zone drone flight took place. UTM coordinate system divides world 60 north-south zones, 6 degrees longitude wide. calculate UTM zone flight took place, can use rough longitude flight took place apply formula: \[ UTM_{zone} = \lfloor \frac{Longitude + 180}{6} \rfloor + 1 \] case, average longitude flight used example -119.45 UTM zone : Next need define hemisphere flight took place either north south. case, example flight took place northern hemisphere: Lastly, need define number decimal digits coordinate. Using decimal coordinate system (.e., -119.45 instead coordinate systems like DMS 119° 27’ 0” W highly discourage) decimal degree convey certain distance. distances differ longitude latitude degrees become smaller higher latitudes. part documentation package included data set (spatial_resolution_lat_long.csv) visualize pattern. plot generated data express area rectangle sides (representing longitude latitude) specific number decimal digits (.e., 1, 0.1, 0.001 etc.) different latitudes.  seen , recommend spatial resolution 5 decimal places since, depending latitude, convey area smaller 1 m^2 relevant scale vast majority terrestrial ectotherms:

what: Step 3: Transforming coordinates and generating final output
title: Reading & processing .TIF drone files
text: pointed earlier, coordinate columns final df obtained previous step weird format. Currently, x y defined distance meters western northern edges UTM zone flight took place. correlate information data sources, need transform data decimal coordinate system. , first need define projection string, provide information function project proj4 package perform transformation. read_n_process_tif function , happening behind scenes: defined string can project data decimal coordinate units. lengthiest step entire process. seen , coordinates now much easier interpret. Last least need define spatial resolution conducting measurements dependent amount decimal_places defined earlier. last summarizing step reduces size data frame substantially point data can already plotted using ggplot tools part posterior analysis. image one plotted earlier recovered
code: # generate the string including utm_zone and hemisphere string <-  paste("+proj=utm +zone=", utm_zone," +", hemisphere," +ellps=WGS84 +datum=WGS84 +units=m +no_defs", sep = "") string ## [1] "+proj=utm +zone=11 +north +ellps=WGS84 +datum=WGS84 +units=m +no_defs" # project to decimal coordinates coords <- as.data.frame(project(data.frame(x = df$x, y = df$y), string, inverse = TRUE)) head(coords) ##           x        y ## 1 -119.4595 39.75115 ## 2 -119.4595 39.75115 ## 3 -119.4595 39.75115 ## 4 -119.4595 39.75115 ## 5 -119.4595 39.75115 ## 6 -119.4595 39.75115 # add temperature column to coordinates  coords$temp <- df$layer  # rename x & y columns  data <- coords %>% rename(long = x, lat = y)  # define decimal degrees on latitude and longitude measurements data$long <- as.numeric(format(round(data$long, decimal_places), nsmall = decimal_places)) data$lat <- as.numeric(format(round(data$lat, decimal_places), nsmall = decimal_places))  # get mean temperature for same coordinates  data <- data %>% group_by(lat, long) %>% summarise(temp = mean(temp, na.rm = T)) ## `summarise()` has grouped output by 'lat'. You can override using the `.groups` ## argument. data ## # A tibble: 105,045 × 3 ## # Groups:   lat [385] ##      lat  long  temp ##    <dbl> <dbl> <dbl> ##  1  39.7 -119.  33.9 ##  2  39.7 -119.  31.9 ##  3  39.7 -119.  30.5 ##  4  39.7 -119.  33.6 ##  5  39.7 -119.  35.0 ##  6  39.7 -119.  33.7 ##  7  39.7 -119.  33.7 ##  8  39.7 -119.  32.7 ##  9  39.7 -119.  33.5 ## 10  39.7 -119.  28.1 ## # … with 105,035 more rows data %>%    ungroup() %>%   ggplot(aes(x = long, y = lat, fill = temp)) +   geom_tile() +   scale_fill_gradient(low = "lightgoldenrod1", high = "red4") +   xlab("Longitude") + ylab("Latitude") +   theme_minimal() +   theme(legend.position = c(0.95, 0.85))

title: Thermal Drone OTM Integration
text: Welcome throne! R package integrate operative temperature model (OTM) thermal drone imaging.

what: Get UTM zone from a longitude value — get_utm_zone
title: Get UTM zone from a longitude value — get_utm_zone
text: Returns universal transverse mercator (UTM) projection zone given longitudinal value

title: Get UTM zone from a longitude value — get_utm_zone
Get UTM zone from a longitude value — get_utm_zone
text: longitude decimal longitude value 180 -180. recommend average longitude site.

title: Get UTM zone from a longitude value — get_utm_zone
text: integer 1 60 indicating UTM zone

title: Get UTM zone from a longitude value — get_utm_zone
text: Determined using equation: $$UTM_{zone} = \lfloor \frac{longitude + 180}{6} \rfloor + 1$$

title: Get UTM zone from a longitude value — get_utm_zone
code: get_utm_zone(longitude = -119.45) #> Error in get_utm_zone(longitude = -119.45): could not find function "get_utm_zone" get_utm_zone(longitude = 2) #> Error in get_utm_zone(longitude = 2): could not find function "get_utm_zone"

title: Hello, World! — hello
text: Prints 'Hello, world!'.
